title: Mental Models
summary: Useful simplifications that help you reason about how the world works.
explanation: A mental model is a compact representation of a system. It leaves details out on purpose so you can focus on the relationships that matter for a decision.
example: A subway map distorts real geography but preserves stations and connections. It is inaccurate in the ways that do not matter and useful in the ways that do.
why: A varied set of models gives you more than one way to frame a problem and helps you notice when a familiar lens is misleading you.

title: Systems Thinking
summary: See patterns and relationships instead of isolated events.
explanation: Systems thinking asks how parts interact over time. It looks for feedback, delays, constraints, and incentives that create recurring behavior.
example: Adding a road lane may reduce traffic briefly. Easier driving attracts more drivers, so congestion returns. The road and driver behavior are one connected system.
why: It shifts your attention from quick fixes toward the structure producing a problem, where smaller and more durable changes are often possible.

title: Feedback Loops
summary: Outputs circle back to influence what happens next.
explanation: Reinforcing loops amplify change, while balancing loops resist it. Delays inside a loop can make a system oscillate or overshoot.
example: A thermostat is a balancing loop: a rising temperature switches the heater off. Compound interest is reinforcing: growth creates a larger base for more growth.
why: Feedback loops explain habits, product growth, climate systems, algorithms, and why some interventions create the opposite of what was intended.

title: Opportunity Cost
summary: Every choice also costs the best alternative you give up.
explanation: Opportunity cost makes tradeoffs visible. The cost of an action includes not only money or time spent, but the value of the strongest option you did not choose.
example: An hour-long meeting costs the hour plus the best alternative use of it, such as focused work, exercise, or rest.
why: It improves choices about attention, products, careers, and money by forcing alternatives into the same comparison.

title: Second-Order Effects
summary: Ask what happens after the obvious result.
explanation: First-order thinking considers the immediate effect. Second-order thinking follows how people and systems adapt, then examines the effects of those adaptations.
example: Rewarding a support team for shorter calls lowers call time, but unresolved issues create repeat calls. The metric improves while the system gets worse.
why: Many poor decisions look good at first. Looking one step further reveals delayed costs, hidden benefits, and unintended behavior.

title: Incentives
summary: Systems produce the behavior they reward, not the behavior they request.
explanation: Incentives are signals and consequences that make actions more or less attractive. They can be financial, social, moral, or embedded in the design of a process.
example: If a school rewards attendance but not learning, it may fill seats without improving understanding. The measured target shapes behavior.
why: Reading incentives helps explain organizations, markets, online platforms, and gaps between stated goals and actual outcomes.

title: Compounding
summary: Small repeated gains can produce nonlinear growth.
explanation: Compounding occurs when gains become part of the base that creates future gains. The same structure can amplify debt, technical shortcuts, and other costs.
example: Ten minutes of learning each day seems small, but every idea makes later ideas easier to learn. Knowledge begins to build on knowledge.
why: It explains why consistency matters in learning, money, health, relationships, and software maintenance.

title: Expected Value
summary: Compare uncertain choices by weighting outcomes by probability.
explanation: Expected value multiplies each possible outcome by its probability, then adds the results. It is a long-run average, not a promise about one attempt.
example: A 20% chance to gain $100 and an 80% chance to lose $10 has an expected value of $12: 0.2 x 100 plus 0.8 x -10.
why: It gives you a consistent language for decisions under uncertainty, from experiments and investments to product bets.

title: Bayesian Updating
summary: Revise a belief as new evidence arrives.
explanation: Bayesian updating starts with a prior belief, weighs how likely the evidence is under competing explanations, and produces a revised belief.
example: A positive medical test is less convincing when a condition is very rare. The base rate matters alongside the test's accuracy.
why: It helps you avoid overreacting to a single observation and makes uncertainty something you can update rather than ignore.

title: Gradient Descent
summary: Improve a model by repeatedly stepping toward lower error.
explanation: Gradient descent measures how a loss function changes, then adjusts parameters in the direction that reduces loss. The learning rate controls the size of each step.
example: Imagine hiking downhill in fog. You feel the local slope, take a small downhill step, and repeat until the ground stops descending.
why: It is the optimization engine behind much of modern machine learning and makes model training less mysterious.

title: Causal Inference
summary: Separate correlation from evidence that one thing caused another.
explanation: Causal inference asks what would have happened under a different action. Because we cannot observe both realities at once, we use experiments or careful assumptions to estimate the counterfactual.
example: Customers who use a feature may retain longer, but perhaps highly engaged customers were more likely to try it. An experiment can separate selection from impact.
why: It prevents confident but costly decisions based on patterns that do not survive intervention.

title: Machine Learning
summary: Build systems that improve predictions from examples.
explanation: Machine learning finds parameterized patterns that generalize from training data to new cases. Good evaluation matters as much as fitting the model.
example: A spam filter learns from labeled messages, but it is useful only if it catches new spam without blocking legitimate mail.
why: Understanding the training, evaluation, and deployment loop helps you reason clearly about what AI systems can and cannot do.

title: Game Theory
summary: Reason about choices when outcomes depend on other decision-makers.
explanation: Game theory studies strategies in situations where each participant's result depends on everyone's actions, information, and incentives.
example: Two competing stores may both prefer higher prices, yet each has an incentive to undercut the other. Their individual logic creates a shared outcome.
why: It clarifies negotiation, pricing, platform design, cooperation, and why individually rational actions can create poor group outcomes.

title: Neural Networks
summary: Layers of simple functions learn useful representations.
explanation: A neural network composes many weighted transformations. Training adjusts those weights so intermediate layers discover features useful for the final task.
example: An image network may first detect edges, then textures and shapes, before combining those signals into an object prediction.
why: Neural networks power modern language, vision, and audio systems. Their layered structure explains both their flexibility and their opacity.

title: Exponential Growth
summary: A constant rate of change creates an accelerating curve.
explanation: Exponential growth means a quantity changes in proportion to its current size. Equal time intervals multiply the value rather than adding a fixed amount.
example: If a quantity doubles every week, it moves from 1 to 2 to 4 to 8. The early steps look quiet even though later growth is dramatic.
why: It builds intuition for epidemics, adoption, interest, compute, and any process where current scale affects future change.
